food_name: Larb Gai (Thai-Lao Spiced Minced Chicken Salad)
food_subgroup: Cooked minced chicken salad dressed with fish sauce, lime juice, dried chilli flakes, toasted ground rice, mint, and shallots

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Larb Gai. Closest parent is a Southeast Asian minced meat salad (FOODON:03315595 — meat salad). No exact URI available; null assigned pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked minced chicken salad dressed with lime juice, fish sauce, dried chilli, toasted ground rice (khao khua), mint, and shallots — closest parent match in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Larb Gai; A0GPF (poultry meat, cooked) used as closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Larb Gai or Thai minced chicken salad as of 2026.

preparation_method: dressed with lime juice and fish sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Larb Gai without rice weighs approximately 180g, delivering around 202 kcal, 29.7g protein, and 10.4g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Larb Gai — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central (Ground Chicken, Cooked, SR Legacy #05298) for macro cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Table for Larb Gai (cooked minced chicken salad, Thai/Lao style). Core macros cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked ground chicken (NDB #05298) adjusted for fish sauce, lime juice, toasted rice, and herb additions. Values are consistent with the composite dish profile. FAO/INFOODS SEA is an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on mixed cooked chicken dish with lime juice, herbs, and small quantity of toasted rice
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Larb Gai in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed cooked chicken salad with acidic dressing (lime juice). Lime juice acidity is documented to suppress post-prandial glucose response (Liljeberg & Bjorck, 1998, AJCN). Toasted ground rice (khao khua) contributes minimal carbohydrate at typical serving quantities (~5g per 100g dish). GL per 180g serving = (45 × 10.4g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.7, rounded to 2.6 per 100g basis. GL per serving = 4.7. The low GI estimate is conservative and clinically defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce (582mg/100g; ~1,048mg per 180g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit or avoid Larb Gai as prepared, or specifically request reduced fish sauce; a 180g serving delivers approximately 1,048mg sodium — exceeding half the WHO daily limit of 2,000mg — and lime juice can be increased to compensate for reduced fish sauce without adding sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (5.8g/100g), high protein (16.5g/100g), low estimated GI (~45), and lime juice acidity suppressing post-prandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Larb Gai is an excellent choice for patients with Type 2 Diabetes: a 180g serving delivers only 10.4g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 4.7, and the high lean protein content (29.7g per serving) supports satiety and glycaemic stability; sodium management remains important if the patient also has hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable lipid profile (low saturated fat 0.8g/100g, zero trans fat) but high sodium (582mg/100g) is a cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lean chicken protein, minimal saturated fat, and anti-inflammatory herbs (mint, chilli capsaicin) make Larb Gai a heart-friendly protein dish, but the high sodium from fish sauce is a cardiovascular concern and patients with CVD should request reduced fish sauce or consume this dish infrequently.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory herb profile (mint rosmarinic acid, capsaicin), high niacin B3 (8.5mg/100g), vitamin B6 (0.65mg/100g), vitamin C (18mg/100g), and lean protein with minimal saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Larb Gai is clinically beneficial for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its combination of anti-inflammatory capsaicin, mint rosmarinic acid, collagen-supporting vitamin C, and immune-modulating B vitamins from lean chicken, with no pro-inflammatory saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Larb Gai (ลาบไก่) is one of the most distinctive salads in the Thai and Lao culinary tradition — a warm, herb-packed minced chicken salad that is simultaneously sour, salty, spicy, and herbal. Minced chicken (coarsely ground breast or thigh) is cooked with fish sauce and a squeeze of lime, then tossed while still warm with toasted ground rice (khao khua — raw rice dry-toasted in a pan until golden-nutty, then ground coarsely), dried chilli flakes, sliced raw shallots, spring onion, and a large handful of fresh mint leaves. The toasted ground rice is the defining textural signature of larb — it adds a subtle nuttiness and gentle crunch that is unique to this dish. Larb is considered the national dish of Laos and the defining dish of Thai Isaan (Northeastern) cuisine. In Singapore, it is available at Thai Isaan-style restaurants and some hawker Thai stalls. Nutritionally, Larb Gai is exceptional: very high protein (16.5g/100g), low carbohydrate (5.8g/100g), low estimated GI (~45), and packed with anti-inflammatory herbs. The high sodium from fish sauce (582mg/100g) is the primary clinical caution, particularly for patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, Larb Gai is an outstanding choice — a 180g serving delivers only 10.4g carbohydrate and 29.7g protein. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal when prepared with certified halal chicken and fish sauce.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA Table for Larb Gai, an approved regional source for Southeast Asian dishes. Core macros cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked ground chicken. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement exists for Larb Gai in the University of Sydney GI Database); confidence is therefore medium rather than high. All values are internally consistent and clinically plausible.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Larb Gai as a composite dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level; individual fatty acid breakdown not available. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Table. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — lean chicken and plant-based dressing contain no industrial trans fats; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked ground chicken). GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed cooked chicken salad with acidic lime dressing; no direct GI measurement for Larb Gai exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. GL per 180g serving = 4.7. Glycemic load per 100g basis = 2.6. Sodium caution: a 180g serving delivers ~1,048mg sodium from fish sauce — clinically significant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. Thai dish: Thai-script local name (ลาบไก่) is mandatory and present. Hindi local name is not applicable for this Thai dish and is left null.

Added cardiovascular_disease health context (missing from input).

Expanded aliases_by_language with all required language keys and verified consistency with flat aliases array.
